name: Shopona
description: Shopona, also known as Soponna, Sakpata, or Shapana, is a significant and feared deity within the Yoruba pantheon, primarily associated with smallpox and infectious diseases. He is a powerful and complex figure, representing not only the destructive force of illness but also the potential for healing and control over disease. Reverence for Shopona is deeply rooted in West African cultures, particularly among the Yoruba people of Nigeria, Benin, and Togo. His worship involves elaborate rituals and ceremonies aimed at appeasing his wrath and seeking his protection against outbreaks. The priests of Shopona held considerable influence, traditionally controlling access to medicines and possessing the knowledge to manage epidemics. Shopona's power is often linked to the earth, further emphasizing his connection to life and death cycles. His worship reflects a deep understanding of disease and the importance of maintaining balance in the community to avoid his devastating impact. The deity's ambiguous nature underscores the precarious relationship between humanity and the forces of nature.
subtitle: God of Smallpox

keyMyths.title: The Origin of Smallpox
keyMyths.description: According to Yoruba mythology, Shopona was once a human being who was afflicted with a terrible skin disease. He was ostracized by his community and forced to live in isolation. Embittered by his experience, he sought power from the divine realm and transformed himself into a deity capable of inflicting the same suffering he had endured. This myth explains the origin of smallpox and the reasons for Shopona's vengeful nature, highlighting the consequences of social exclusion and the search for power.
keyMyths.source: Bascom, William. *The Yoruba of Southwestern Nigeria*. Stanford University Press, 1969.

keyMyths.title: Shopona and the Farmers
keyMyths.description: This myth tells the story of a village that neglected to pay proper respect to Shopona. As a result, he unleashed a devastating epidemic of smallpox upon their crops and livestock. The villagers, desperate to appease him, offered sacrifices and performed elaborate rituals. Shopona, moved by their remorse, eventually relented and restored the health of their land, demonstrating the importance of honoring the deities and maintaining balance in the natural world.
keyMyths.source: Idowu, E. Bolaji. *Olódùmarè: God in Yoruba Belief*. Longman, 1962.

keyMyths.title: The Priest's Dilemma
keyMyths.description: In this tale, a priest of Shopona is faced with a moral dilemma. He possesses the power to heal the sick but also the knowledge to spread disease. He must choose between using his abilities for good or evil, ultimately deciding to prioritize the well-being of his community. This myth explores the ethical responsibilities of those who wield power and the importance of using it wisely, emphasizing the duality inherent in Shopona's nature.
keyMyths.source: Awolalu, J. Omosade. *Yoruba Beliefs and Sacrificial Rites*. Longman, 1979.

sources.author: Bascom, William
